chain_of_thought: The problem requires mutual exclusion for N processes, so the core state variable is a function mapping each process in 1..N to its current phase: idle, trying, or critical. This three-valued enum is necessary and sufficient because the protocol has exactly three observable phases per process -- not yet competing, requesting entry, and inside the critical section. The state space is bounded because each of N processes independently occupies one of three discrete states, giving 3^N reachable configurations. The idle-to-trying transition models a process announcing intent to enter; the trying-to-critical transition models actual entry, guarded by the conjunct that no other process is already critical (this is where mutual exclusion is enforced at the action level); the critical-to-idle transition models release. The TypeOK invariant restricts the state function's range, while the implicit safety property -- at most one process in critical -- follows directly from the guard on Enter: a process can only move to critical when all others are not critical, so two simultaneous critical states are unreachable from Init under the Next relation.

The dining philosophers problem requires two state variables: philosophers (a function 1..N -> {thinking, hungry, eating}) tracking each philosopher's phase, and forks (a function 1..N -> BOOLEAN) tracking whether each shared fork is available. Two variables are needed because fork availability is not derivable from philosopher states alone -- a philosopher becomes hungry before acquiring forks, so state and resource ownership are decoupled. The circular topology is encoded by leftFork(i) and rightFork(i) using modular arithmetic wrapping 1-to-N and N-to-1. The state space is bounded: N philosophers each in 3 states times N forks each in 2 states gives 3^N * 2^N configurations. Three actions model the real-world events: Hungry(i) transitions thinking-to-hungry without touching forks; PickUp(i) atomically acquires both adjacent forks when free, transitioning hungry-to-eating and marking forks FALSE; PutDown(i) releases both forks, transitioning eating-to-thinking. The Safety invariant states no two adjacent philosophers eat simultaneously, which is sufficient because PickUp requires both shared forks to be free, and adjacent eaters would require the same fork to be held by two philosophers -- impossible since forks are set to FALSE atomically on acquisition.

This variant of dining philosophers uses an ownership-tracking encoding for forks: forks is a function 1..N -> 0..N where 0 means free and a positive value p means philosopher p holds that fork. This is richer than a Boolean encoding because it lets the spec directly assert which philosopher owns which fork, enabling stronger type invariants. The states variable tracks each philosopher in {thinking, hungry, eating}. LeftFork and RightFork use the standard circular index wrapping. The Hungry action models a philosopher declaring intent without acquiring resources. The Eat action is guarded by both adjacent forks being 0 (free), then atomically sets both fork entries to the philosopher's ID and transitions to eating -- this atomic two-fork acquisition avoids partial-grab deadlock at the specification level. The Think action requires the philosopher to currently own both forks (verified via forks[LeftFork(p)] = p), then releases them by resetting to 0. The state space is bounded by N * (N+1)^N * 3^N. TypeOK captures this structure, ensuring fork values stay in 0..N and states in the three-element set.

This dining philosophers variant uses a self-ownership convention for free forks: forks[i] = i means fork i is free (owned by its home position), while forks[i] = j for j != i means philosopher j holds it. This avoids introducing 0 as a sentinel. The CanEat(i) predicate checks that both the left fork (indexed i) and right fork (indexed Right(i)) are at their home positions. PickUp atomically claims both forks by setting them to i and transitions to eating. PutDown returns each fork to its home position (forks[i] = i, forks[Right(i)] = Right(i)) and transitions to thinking. BecomeHungry transitions thinking-to-hungry without modifying forks. The state space is bounded: forks ranges over [1..N -> 1..N] and states over [1..N -> 3 values], giving N^N * 3^N states. The weak fairness WF condition on Next ensures liveness -- every continuously enabled action eventually fires, preventing indefinite starvation. Mutual exclusion of adjacent eaters is guaranteed because PickUp requires both forks at home positions, and any eating philosopher has moved those forks away from home.

The mutual exclusion problem for N processes requires a single state variable pc mapping each process in Procs (1..N) to one of three program counter values: idle, trying, or critical. A single function suffices because each process's behavior is fully characterized by its phase in the protocol. The ASSUME N in 1..10 bounds the constant for TLC model checking. Three named actions decompose the protocol: TryEnter(p) models a process requesting the critical section; Enter(p) models actual entry, guarded by the universal quantifier ensuring no other process is critical; Exit(p) models release back to idle. The MutualExclusion invariant directly encodes the safety requirement: for any two distinct processes, they cannot both be in critical simultaneously. This invariant is sufficient because it is exactly the negation of the unsafe state, and it is preserved by Next because Enter's guard explicitly checks for the absence of any other critical process before allowing entry.

Two-phase commit requires three state variables: coordState tracking the coordinator's phase (init/waiting/committed/aborted), partState mapping each participant to its local state (working/prepared/aborted/committed), and decision recording the global outcome (none/commit/abort). Three variables are necessary because the coordinator's state, each participant's state, and the commit decision evolve semi-independently -- participants vote asynchronously, and the decision is a separate global fact. The protocol phases map to actions: Prepare moves the coordinator from init to waiting; VoteYes/VoteNo model each participant's response; DecideCommit fires only when all participants are prepared (the unanimity requirement); DecideAbort fires when any participant aborted. CommitPart and AbortPart propagate the decision to individual participants. The Consistency invariant states that any committed participant implies decision = commit, which is sufficient because DecideCommit only fires when all votes are prepared, and CommitPart only fires when decision = commit, so no participant can reach committed unless the unanimous vote occurred.

Lamport's bakery algorithm requires three state variables: num (ticket numbers), flag (doorway flags), and pc (program counters). The num variable maps each process to its drawn ticket number (0 means inactive); flag indicates whether a process is currently in the doorway (choosing a number); pc tracks the protocol phase (idle/doorway/waiting/critical). The Doorway action draws a ticket by computing Max of all current tickets plus one, and sets the flag to TRUE to signal the doorway is being traversed. FinishDoorway clears the flag. EnterCS is guarded by two conditions for each other process: the other's flag is FALSE (not mid-doorway) and either the other has no ticket or the current process has a strictly smaller ticket (with process ID as tiebreaker). The bound Max < 18 on ticket values ensures finite state space for TLC (20^N * 2^N * 4^N states). The MutualExclusion invariant holds because two processes in critical would require each to have passed the other's doorway check, but the ticket ordering with tiebreaker is a total order, making simultaneous passage impossible.

A key-value store needs two state variables: store (the actual map from keys to values) and lastGet (recording the most recent Get result per key). The store variable is necessary to track the current database state; lastGet is necessary to express the linearizability property as a state predicate rather than a trace property. Both map Keys (1..N) to Values (1..N) union {0}, where 0 represents the initial empty/unset state. Put(k,v) updates store[k] to v; Get(k) copies store[k] into lastGet[k]. The state space is bounded because both keys and values come from finite sets 1..N plus the sentinel 0, giving (N+1)^(2N) states. The Linearizability invariant asserts that for every key, lastGet[k] equals either store[k] or 0 (the initial default). This is sufficient because Get atomically reads the current store value, so lastGet can never hold a stale non-zero value that disagrees with store -- it either reflects the latest Put or has never been read.

Single-decree Paxos requires four state variables: maxBal (highest ballot each acceptor has promised), maxVBal (highest ballot at which each acceptor accepted a value), maxVal (the value accepted at that ballot), and chosen (the globally chosen value, -1 if none). These four are necessary because Paxos separates the promise phase (tracked by maxBal) from the accept phase (tracked by maxVBal/maxVal), and the chosen value is a derived global fact. Quorum is defined as any subset of acceptors with more than half the population, ensuring any two quorums overlap. Prepare(b) models Phase 1a/1b: a proposer gets a quorum to promise ballot b by advancing their maxBal. Accept(b,v) models Phase 2a/2b: given a quorum that promised b, the proposer picks v consistent with the highest previously accepted value (or freely if none), and the quorum accepts. Choose models learning: when a quorum has accepted the same value, it becomes chosen. The Consistency invariant (chosen in Values when set) is maintained because quorum overlap ensures any two Accept actions on different ballots must agree on the value, preventing divergence.

The bounded retransmission protocol requires six state variables: sChunk (sender's current chunk index), rChunk (receiver's last received chunk), retry (current retry count), sDone/rDone (sender/receiver completion status), and channel (modeling the unreliable medium). Six variables are needed because the protocol tracks sender progress, receiver progress, retry budget, both sides' completion status, and the channel state independently. The channel is modeled as a single-message buffer with five states: empty, data, lost, ack, ack_lost. Send places data on the channel; Deliver moves data to receiver and produces an ack; Lose and LoseAck model message loss; RecvAck advances the sender to the next chunk; Timeout handles lost messages by either retrying (incrementing retry up to MAX) or declaring failure. The state space is bounded by N chunks, MAX retries, and the finite channel states. The DeliveryOrFailure invariant states that sDone=ok implies all chunks were received (rChunk=N), which holds because RecvAck only sets sDone=ok when sChunk=N and a valid ack was received, meaning the last chunk was successfully delivered.

Snapshot isolation requires four state variables: db (the committed database state), txState (each transaction's lifecycle phase), txSnapshot (the database snapshot each transaction reads from), and txWrites (the set of keys each transaction has written). Four variables are needed because SI decouples read visibility (snapshot) from write tracking (write set), and both are per-transaction. Begin(t) captures the current db into txSnapshot[t], giving the transaction a consistent read view. Write(t,k) adds k to the write set without modifying db -- writes are buffered. Commit(t) checks that no already-committed transaction has a conflicting write set (the write-write conflict detection that defines SI), then applies buffered writes to db. Abort(t) simply marks the transaction as aborted without modifying db. The state space is bounded: db has values in 0..5 per key, txState has 4 phases per transaction, txWrites is a subset of N keys. The NoWriteConflict invariant -- no two committed transactions share written keys -- is sufficient because the Commit guard explicitly checks for disjoint write sets against all previously committed transactions before allowing the commit to proceed.

Clock synchronization requires two state variables: clock (mapping each node to its current clock value) and synced (tracking whether each node has completed its synchronization round). The clock variable is initialized nondeterministically from [Nodes -> 0..3] to model initial clock drift. The synced variable is necessary to enforce a single-round protocol: each node synchronizes exactly once. The Sync(i) action picks an arbitrary other node j, computes the integer average of their clocks, and updates node i's clock to that average, then marks i as synced. Integer division introduces rounding but preserves boundedness within 0..10. The Done action is a stuttering step for when all nodes have synced. The state space is bounded because clocks range over 0..10 and synced is Boolean per node. The ClockBound invariant asserts that after all nodes have synced, any two clocks differ by at most 5, which is sufficient because pairwise averaging with initial spread of at most 3 cannot diverge clocks beyond the bounded range, and the -5..5 bound is conservative for this initial condition.

A memory allocator needs two state variables: free (the set of currently unallocated pages) and allocated (a function mapping each client to the set of pages it holds). Two variables are needed because the allocator must track both the pool of available pages and per-client ownership. Init sets free = Pages (all pages available) and allocated to empty sets for all clients. Allocate(c) picks a page from the free pool, removes it from free, and adds it to allocated[c] -- modeling a client requesting a page. Release(c) picks a page from allocated[c], removes it, and returns it to free -- modeling a client freeing a page. The state space is bounded because Pages is finite (1..N) and each page is either free or allocated to exactly one client, giving at most (N+1)^N configurations. The SafeAllocation invariant asserts that for any two distinct clients, their allocated sets are disjoint, which is sufficient because Allocate only draws from free (which is disjoint from all allocated sets by construction), so a page can never appear in two clients' sets simultaneously.

The pub-sub broker requires three state variables: subscriptions (mapping each subscriber to its set of subscribed topics), published (counting messages published per topic), and delivered (counting per-subscriber-per-topic deliveries). Three variables are needed because subscription registration, message production, and delivery progress are independent concerns. Subscribe(s,t) adds topic t to subscriber s's subscription set. Publish(t) increments the published count for topic t, bounded by MaxMsgs to keep the state space finite. Deliver(s,t) increments delivered[s][t] only if t is in s's subscriptions and delivered count is below published count -- modeling the broker forwarding a message. The state space is bounded by the finite topic/subscriber sets and the MaxMsgs cap on counters. The DeliveryGuarantee invariant states that for any subscriber s subscribed to topic t, delivered[s][t] <= published[t], which is sufficient because Deliver's guard explicitly requires delivered < published before incrementing, so over-delivery is impossible, and the invariant captures that no subscriber receives more messages than were actually published.

Dekker's algorithm for 2 processes requires three state variables: wants (Boolean flags indicating intent to enter), turn (the tiebreaker variable alternating between processes), and pc (a 6-state program counter per process tracking protocol phases). The wants flags are necessary for the flag-based signaling that distinguishes Dekker from simpler lock algorithms. The turn variable resolves contention when both processes want entry simultaneously. The pc tracks: idle (not competing), set_flag (flag raised, about to check), check (inspecting the other's flag), wait (backing off when contention detected and it's not our turn), critical, and exit. The key Dekker logic is in Wait(p): if the other process's flag is raised and it's not p's turn, p backs off by clearing its flag and entering wait; when turn eventually rotates to p (WaitForTurn), it re-raises its flag. ExitCS hands turn to the other process and clears the flag. MutualExclusion holds because entry to critical requires the other's flag to be FALSE (checked in Check and EnterFromCheck), and the turn variable ensures that when both flags are raised, only the process whose turn it is will persist while the other backs off.

This is the Klotski sliding puzzle, where the single state variable board holds the current set of pieces (each piece being a set of grid positions). The board is a set of Piece values where Piece = SUBSET Pos and Pos = (0..W-1) x (0..H-1) for a 4x5 grid. The key operators are: dir(p, es) computes valid von Neumann neighbor directions from position p that lead to occupied (non-empty) cells, filtering out directions that go off-grid or land on other empty squares. move(p, d) finds the piece adjacent to empty position p in direction d using ChooseOne, then translates that entire piece in the opposite direction (toward the empty space). update(e, es) combines these: for each valid direction from empty cell e, it computes the moved piece and validates that the translated piece does not collide with other pieces and stays within bounds, then constructs new board configurations. Init sets the Klotski starting layout (10 pieces including the 2x2 block, four 1x2 blocks, one 2x1 block, and four 1x1 blocks). Next existentially picks an empty cell and transitions to any valid board in update(e, empty). The state space is finite because the grid is 4x5=20 cells with a fixed number of pieces.

This is a completion variant of the Klotski sliding puzzle specification. The board variable holds the current piece configuration as a set of sets of positions on a 4x5 grid. The ChooseOne operator is critical -- it selects the unique piece from board that contains a given position, enabling move(p,d) to identify which piece to slide. The dir operator filters the four cardinal directions to those that are in-bounds and point to occupied cells (not in the empty set es), ensuring we only attempt to move pieces that actually exist adjacent to the gap. The move operator translates the identified piece by the negation of direction d (sliding the piece toward the empty space). The update operator validates moves by checking that the translated piece does not overlap with remaining pieces and stays within Pos bounds, then constructs the resulting board by removing the old piece and inserting the moved one. KlotskiGoal checks whether the 2x2 block has reached the bottom-center exit position. The specification explores all reachable board configurations via BFS when checked with TLC, and the goal predicate can be used as an invariant negation to find the solution path.

The readers-writers problem with fair queuing requires three state variables: readers (set of currently reading actors), writers (set of currently writing actors), and waiting (a sequence of tagged requests). The waiting queue is a sequence of pairs like <<"read", actor>> or <<"write", actor>>, which is essential for FIFO fairness -- a set would lose ordering. Helper operators WaitingToRead and WaitingToWrite extract actor IDs from the queue using SelectSeq filtering. TryRead/TryWrite append to the queue (guarded by not already waiting). ReadOrWrite processes the head of the queue: if it is a read request, the actor joins readers (multiple concurrent readers allowed); if it is a write request, it requires readers = {} (exclusive access). The writers = {} precondition on ReadOrWrite prevents new reads or writes while a writer is active. StopActivity models a reader or writer finishing. The Safety invariant has two parts: readers and writers cannot be simultaneously non-empty (no read-write overlap), and at most one writer exists. These are sufficient because ReadOrWrite only grants write access when readers is empty, and Write only adds one actor to writers at a time. The Fairness conditions using WF ensure liveness -- every actor eventually reads and writes.

This is a completion variant of the readers-writers specification with FIFO queuing. The three state variables -- readers (set), writers (set), and waiting (sequence of tagged pairs) -- separate the concern of who is currently active from who is waiting. The sequence type for waiting is critical: it enforces FIFO ordering so that a write request is not starved by later read requests jumping ahead. The ToSet helper converts the sequence to a set for filtering operations via SelectSeq. The key design decision is that ReadOrWrite dispatches on Head(waiting) with writers = {} as a global precondition -- this means no new access is granted while any writer is active, preventing read-write races. The Read action does not require readers = {} because multiple concurrent readers are safe. The Write action additionally requires readers = {} because a writer needs exclusive access. The StopActivity action uses an IF to dispatch between reader removal and writer removal. The Safety invariant captures both the mutual exclusion between readers and writers and the single-writer constraint, which together ensure the standard readers-writers safety properties are maintained.

The Die Hard water jug puzzle needs exactly two state variables: big (gallons in the 5-gallon jug) and small (gallons in the 3-gallon jug). These two integers fully describe the system state because the jugs and the faucet (infinite source/sink) are the only containers. TypeOK bounds big to 0..5 and small to 0..3, giving 6*4=24 possible states -- a very small state space ideal for exhaustive BFS by TLC. The six actions model all physically meaningful operations: FillSmallJug and FillBigJug model filling from the faucet (setting to capacity); EmptySmallJug and EmptyBigJug model pouring onto the ground (setting to 0); SmallToBig and BigToSmall model pouring between jugs, using Min to compute the result -- the receiving jug gets the lesser of its capacity and the total water in both jugs, and the donor loses exactly what the receiver gained. The Init state has both jugs empty. The invariant NotSolved (big != 4) is intentionally the negation of the goal: TLC's counterexample trace when this invariant is violated reveals the shortest sequence of actions to reach big = 4, effectively solving the puzzle via model checking.
